ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general tempura class; no pumpkin-specific tempura subclass exists. Mapped to the closest broader tempura food product class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for pumpkin tempura. A0FJP (deep-fried vegetable product) used as the closest defensible parent category. Pumpkin tempura is a battered deep-fried vegetable preparation consistent with this category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for pumpkin tempura as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of pumpkin tempura consists of approximately 3 pieces (about 90g), as commonly served as a side dish in Japanese restaurants or as part of a tempura set meal.

nutrient_source.title: Pumpkin Tempura — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database (F14060012)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo record F14060012 for Pumpkin Tempura. All seven reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and micronutrient fields not reported by HPB; set to -1 where unavailable.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pumpkin tempura as a composite dish. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on two analogous approved-source components: (1) pumpkin/squash GI ~75 (University of Sydney GI Database, boiled pumpkin) and (2) tempura batter (refined wheat flour) GI ~70. The batter coating raises the GI of raw pumpkin; however, the fat content from deep-frying (14.79g/100g) slows gastric emptying and moderates the glycaemic response, yielding a composite estimate of GI 65. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 21.87) / 100 ≈ 14.2 (serving = 90g, carbohydrate = 24.3 × 0.9 = 21.87g). This is a medium GL.

hypertension.guidance: Pumpkin tempura has a low sodium content of 53mg/100g and is suitable for hypertension management when consumed without high-sodium dipping sauces such as tentsuyu; patients should be advised to avoid or strictly limit soy-based or mirin-based dipping sauces which can add 200–400mg sodium per tablespoon.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined-carbohydrate load from tempura batter with low dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pumpkin tempura carries a medium-to-high glycaemic impact (estimated GI 65, GL ~14 per 90g serving) driven by the refined wheat flour batter; patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size, avoid mirin-sweetened dipping sauces, and prefer steamed or roasted pumpkin preparations to minimise postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat from deep-frying (14.79g/100g) with unknown saturated and trans fat profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The deep-frying process contributes 14.79g fat per 100g; saturated and trans fat data are not available from the HPB source, but frying oils used in commercial tempura preparation may contain partially hydrogenated fats — patients with cardiovascular disease should limit intake and request information on the frying oil used, favouring preparations using canola or sunflower oil over palm or partially hydrogenated oils.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end products (AGEs)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-fried preparations such as pumpkin tempura are strongly discouraged for rheumatoid arthritis patients because the high-heat frying process generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) that directly exacerbate joint inflammation; patients should substitute with steamed or roasted pumpkin to retain anti-inflammatory beta-carotene benefits without the AGE burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pumpkin tempura (かぼちゃの天ぷら, kabocha no tempura) is a classic Japanese deep-fried dish in which slices of pumpkin — typically kabocha squash — are coated in a light, airy tempura batter made from cold water, wheat flour, and egg, then briefly deep-fried in hot vegetable oil until the batter is pale golden and crisp. The result is a contrast of textures: a delicate, slightly crunchy batter exterior encasing the naturally sweet, dense, and slightly starchy flesh of the pumpkin. Kabocha squash is prized in Japanese cuisine for its rich, chestnut-like sweetness and vibrant orange flesh, which provides beta-carotene and dietary fibre. Per 100g, pumpkin tempura provides 241 kcal, 2.63g protein, 14.79g fat, 24.3g carbohydrate, 5.19g sugar, 1.9g dietary fibre, and 53mg sodium. The relatively high fat content (14.79g/100g) is attributable to oil absorption during deep-frying. The low sodium content (53mg/100g) makes it suitable for hypertension when consumed without dipping sauce. However, the refined wheat flour batter creates a moderate-to-high glycaemic response (estimated GI 65), making portion control important for Type 2 Diabetes management. Pumpkin tempura is commonly served as a side dish (okazu), as part of a tempura set (tempura teishoku), or alongside tempura udon or soba. It is vegetarian but not vegan due to egg in the batter, and contains gluten. Clinically, the deep-frying process generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs), which are pro-inflammatory and contraindicated for rheumatoid arthritis patients.
common_user_queries: How many calories in pumpkin tempura?
Is pumpkin tempura suitable for diabetics?
Can I eat pumpkin tempura if I have high blood pressure?
Is pumpkin tempura good for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo record F14060012 at confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for pumpkin and refined wheat flour; this is documented as an estimate. Multilingual Japanese name is well-established. Overall confidence is high for macros and medium for GI, yielding a composite high confidence rating.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB source): saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Reason: HPB Foodo record F14060012 reports only energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, and dietary fibre; detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrients, and moisture data are not published in this record. USDA FoodData Central does not contain an exact match for pumpkin tempura as a composite dish; a full micronutrient profile would require laboratory analysis or a composite calculation from raw ingredients. GI value of 65 is an analogous estimate derived from University of Sydney GI Database data for boiled pumpkin (GI ~75) and refined wheat flour (GI ~70), moderated downward by the fat content (14.79g/100g) which slows gastric emptying. This estimate should be replaced with a direct measurement if one becomes available. The input record listed the condition key as 'diabetes'; this has been updated to 'type2_diabetes' per Veritas naming rules. Dietary tag 'low_sodium' from input is retained as clinically supported (53mg/100g). Vegan flag set to false because standard tempura batter contains egg.
